video_title: Building the Onchain Brokerage: Glider's Vision for Investing | Brian Huang
gist: Glider co-founder Brian Huang argues the future of investing is programmatic, personalized, chain-abstracted portfolio management built on tokenized real-world assets, aiming to be the 'onchain Fidelity' that abstracts away DeFi plumbing for mostly non-US retail users; he is bearish on LLM-picks-stocks gimmicks and AMM-based tokenized equities, and bullish on real-yield and centrally-issued RWAs.

themes.theme: Tokenization scale and the RWA opportunity

insights.claim: There is roughly $34 billion of tokenized assets onchain across stocks, treasuries, and emerging corporate bonds and mortgage-backed securities, a category Glider describes as growing parabolically.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Frames the addressable market for onchain asset management; watch RWA TVL growth as the leading indicator for products like Glider.
insights.quote: we now have 34 billion of tokenized assets on chain and these are real things

insights.claim: Only about $2 billion of stocks exist onchain today versus a ~$60 trillion US equity market cap, meaning onchain equities are roughly 0.003% penetrated and extremely early.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Quantifies how nascent tokenized equity liquidity is; a long runway but also a constraint on what portfolios can be built today.
insights.quote: there's about two billion dollars of onchain stocks um in existence today. The US uh market cap of equities is 60 trillion. That's like 0.00 two to three%

insights.claim: 92% of Glider's users are outside the US, using the product as their primary access point to US stocks and treasuries they otherwise cannot buy.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: The real near-term demand for tokenized US equities is international access, not US users seeking yield; sizing TAM should center on offshore retail.
insights.quote: 92% of our users are outside of the US. These are folks who don't have access to these assets

themes.theme: Tokenized-equity liquidity models and issuer selection

insights.claim: Glider prefers partners like Ono that source liquidity from traditional markets and mint on demand (enabling ~$20M clips with little price impact) and avoids AMM-based issuers like X Stocks where prices can dislocate ~4% from the underlying.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Liquidity model is the key differentiator among tokenized-equity issuers; AMM-based tokens carry tracking-error risk that makes them unsuitable for spot long-term holders.
insights.quote: whereas Coinbase might be trading at 200 bucks or whatever on NASDAQ, it might be at 192 on uh on an AMM and that's a big problem. So we don't like that model

insights.claim: Permissioned onchain assets with no DeFi composability are essentially pointless 'digital receipt tokens' — like 'tokenizing a pencil' nobody can borrow or lend.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: A filter for evaluating institutional tokenization announcements: composability/interoperability, not mere issuance, is what creates value.
insights.quote: it would be like putting a pencil on the blockchain and being like, "My pencil's tokenized."

insights.claim: Stock perpetuals are not retail-ready because funding rates can be extreme and most retail does not understand margin, so spot tokenized exposure suits long-horizon holders better.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Perps are over-hyped as the answer to onchain equity exposure; spot tokenized stocks are the right product for buy-and-hold retail.
insights.quote: if you look at onchain perpetuals or rather stock perpetuals the funding rates are sometimes absolutely crazy and so it's just not a product that is ready for retail

themes.theme: Automated investing, AI and the execution engine

insights.claim: Handing money to an LLM to pick stocks has no long-term alpha — it is noise/beta — and anyone with real LLM trading alpha would run a trading firm rather than give it away.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: A skeptical filter for 'AI agent invests for you' pitches; real product value is in deterministic execution, not the model's stock picks.
insights.quote: If anybody actually had really good alpha and some type of like LLM trading strategy, they would build a trading firm.

insights.claim: Glider's edge is a deterministic execution 'sandbox' that separates intelligence (any model) from execution, citing Banker as an example of losing money by letting an LLM decide without deterministic rails.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating onchain automated-investing startups should probe execution determinism, not just AI marketing.
insights.quote: Separating out the intelligence... from the actual execution, which darn sure better be accurate and and done properly on chain.

insights.claim: Glider does not commingle user funds — each ETF/strategy instance runs in a fully segregated non-custodial wallet — explicitly contrasting with Drift, which lost millions because funds were pooled in one place.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Architecture choice that reduces honeypot/exploit risk; a due-diligence point distinguishing pooled-vault from segregated-account onchain products.
insights.quote: Drift lost millions of dollars because it was all in one place.

insights.claim: Investing is evolving mutual funds to ETFs to direct indexing to fully automated personalized portfolios, where users hold underlying assets that auto-rebalance on rules and natural-language workflows.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: A roadmap thesis for where retail brokerage is heading; public.com cited as the offchain leader to benchmark against.
insights.quote: we're going from direct indexing to what I would argue is fully automated personalized investing

insights.claim: Glider runs a B2B arm embedding curated portfolios (e.g., a Berkshire Hathaway portfolio, a healthcare ETF) into wallets like Base wallets as a new revenue stream for those wallets.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Distribution-via-wallets is a parallel monetization path; relevant to wallet incumbents seeking revenue beyond perps/prediction markets.

insights.claim: Perps and prediction-market integrations are 'fairly saturated' and some wallets wasted time embedding them without the expected returns, whereas durable value lies in long-term asset management.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Counter-consensus on the popular wallet feature race; suggests diminishing returns to chasing perps/prediction-market trends.
insights.quote: some have had what I would argue they've wasted a lot of time trying to embed them and not actually seeing the returns that they would have expected

insights.claim: Glider plans to launch a Mag 7 ETF marketed on a ~5% dividend, and pitches lending tokenized Nvidia stock to earn ~5% yield as a fundamentally better, onchain-only version of holding the stock.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Yield-on-equities is the wedge to attract users who already have stock access; watch whether stock-lending/staking yields materialize at scale.
insights.quote: would you rather hold Nvidia stock or hold Nvidia stock that earns 5% because you can lend it out

insights.claim: The winning onchain UX must abstract away gas, networks, bridging, and token duplicates — presenting one USDC, one Ethereum, and treating wrapped/exchange-branded tokens as the base asset.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Chain/token abstraction is the differentiator for onboarding non-crypto-native users; a benchmark for evaluating consumer onchain apps.
insights.quote: if I told a normal person that you have to bridge USDC to USDC, that doesn't make any sense to a normal person

themes.theme: Yield, risk curation and the centralized-issuer thesis

insights.claim: Glider positions itself as a 'curator of curators,' offering conservative default-on yield (e.g., auto-lending USDC, JupSOL, sUSDC, sUSDS) via vetted Morpho/Kamino-style curators rather than asking users to pick risky high-APR options.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Risk-curation layering is the consumer-safe model for DeFi yield; default-on yield is a UX and retention lever.
insights.quote: I like to describe ourselves as curators of curators.

insights.claim: Onchain vault insurance is uneconomic today — insuring a 7%-yield vault can cost ~6.5% of the yield — so Glider relies on conservative vetting rather than insurance.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Explains why onchain insurance hasn't scaled; insurance economics are a key gating factor for institutional onchain adoption.
insights.quote: if you wanted to insure a vault that is producing 7% yield, you're going to pay out 6 and a half% of the yield to the insurer

insights.claim: Real yield generated offchain by centralized issuers (e.g., Circle, which can freeze/nullify exploited tokens) is far less risky than purely decentralized yield, pointing toward a 'CeDeFi' future of issuer-controlled assets.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: A directional bet that institutional onchain adoption favors centralized control and reversibility over maximal decentralization.
insights.quote: Circle for example can say, "Oh, North Korea took those tokens. We're just going to nullify those tokens"

insights.claim: Large institutional asset managers are 'crypto washing' — dabbling and tokenizing assets for optics rather than building real interoperable products — and remain exploratory, not committed.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Tempers hype around institutional onchain announcements; the signal to watch is genuine interoperability/composability, not press releases.
insights.quote: a lot of what we see I think from the institutional perspective is what I would describe as like crypto washing

insights.claim: Many chains will not survive; a few winners are consolidating while new entrants like Hood Chain, Arc, and Tempo are unproven, and adding any chain carries real operational cost (RPC infrastructure).
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: A consolidation call on L1/L2s; new app-chains face a high bar to win distribution-layer support.
insights.quote: a lot of these chains are not going to survive

insights.claim: Glider will launch its own vaults (first announcement), with institutional interest already lined up to deposit into one specific automated-strategy vault, plus full chain abstraction across base/arbitrum this summer.
insights.speaker: Brian Huang
insights.so_what: Near-term catalysts for Glider; vertical integration into vaults captures capital-efficiency and yield economics in-house.
insights.quote: we will be launching vaults. So this is the first time that I've announced that we're being doing vaults.
